narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50—which is below the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 31, lower than the usual 36.70. The admissions model predicted a 60% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected; the school uses this prediction to prioritize students likely to succeed and pass the bar right away. This decision hinged mostly on your academic performance in school, particularly how your grades ranked compared to other students. The strongest factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 decile rank of 1, meaning you placed in the bottom tenth of your class that year—far below the average rank of about 5.5—which signaled to the model a significant risk of struggling with the intense demands of law school and the bar. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile rank of 2, again well below the typical 5.7 average, reinforcing concerns about consistent underperformance early on. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA actually nudged the prediction slightly toward success, as the model saw it as a relative strength compared to your later school rankings, though not enough to offset the other red flags. Overall, your ranks placed you below most applicants in key academic metrics, while your full-time status aligned with the vast majority at 92.7%, so it didn't sway things much either way. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many students with similar demographics thrive—but the model focuses heavily on these proven predictors of bar passage. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying to schools with more flexible criteria. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early or explore part-time options if that fits your situation better. With focused improvements in these areas, your odds could shift dramatically for future applications. Keep pushing; many have turned similar profiles around.
